content.text: Telemetry is the systematic collection of operational signals from a running system. For GenAI applications, these signals include request latency, token consumption, estimated cost, error rates, and cache performance. Without telemetry you cannot confidently answer whether your application is working well, where spend is going, or what changed the day a quality regression appeared.
This sub-subtopic shows how to instrument a FastAPI-based LLM backend with OpenTelemetry, store signals in appropriate backends, and query them for dashboards and alerting.

attrs.alt: Telemetry pipeline: LLM request → middleware → OTel exporter → storage backends → dashboard

content.text: Three distinct signal types serve different purposes:
P95 latency, token rate, cost dashboards

All four are complementary. A spike in P95 latency (metric) triggers an alert → you find the offending trace → the log line shows a token-overflow error on a specific model version → the events table reveals which user cohort triggered it.

pip install opentelemetry-sdk \
            opentelemetry-exporter-otlp-proto-grpc \
            opentelemetry-instrumentation-fastapi \
            opentelemetry-instrumentation-httpx

# telemetry.py
from opentelemetry import trace, metrics
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics import MeterProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics.export import PeriodicExportingMetricReader
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc.metric_exporter import OTLPMetricExporter

def configure_telemetry(service_name: str, otlp_endpoint: str) -> None:
    # Traces
    tracer_provider = TracerProvider()
    tracer_provider.add_span_processor(
        BatchSpanProcessor(OTLPSpanExporter(endpoint=otlp_endpoint))
    )
    trace.set_tracer_provider(tracer_provider)

    # Metrics
    reader = PeriodicExportingMetricReader(
        OTLPMetricExporter(endpoint=otlp_endpoint),
        export_interval_millis=15_000,
    )
    metrics.set_meter_provider(MeterProvider(metric_readers=[reader]))

# main.py
from fastapi import FastAPI
from opentelemetry.instrumentation.fastapi import FastAPIInstrumentor
from telemetry import configure_telemetry

configure_telemetry(
    service_name="chat-api",
    otlp_endpoint="http://otel-collector:4317",
)

app = FastAPI()
FastAPIInstrumentor.instrument_app(app)  # auto-instruments all routes
The auto-instrumentation records HTTP spans. Add LLM-specific attributes on each call:

import time
from opentelemetry import trace

tracer = trace.get_tracer("chat-api")
meter  = metrics.get_meter("chat-api")

# Metric instruments (create once at module level)
latency_hist   = meter.create_histogram("llm.request.duration_ms")
input_tok_ctr  = meter.create_counter("llm.tokens.input")
output_tok_ctr = meter.create_counter("llm.tokens.output")
cost_ctr       = meter.create_counter("llm.cost.usd", unit="USD")

INPUT_COST  = {"claude-3-5-haiku-20241022": 0.80,  "claude-opus-4-8": 15.0}   # per 1M tokens
OUTPUT_COST = {"claude-3-5-haiku-20241022": 4.00,  "claude-opus-4-8": 75.0}

async def call_llm(
    pool, model: str, messages: list[dict], user_id: str, conversation_id: str
) -> dict:
    attrs = {"llm.model": model, "user.id": user_id, "conversation.id": conversation_id}
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("llm.chat", attributes=attrs) as span:
        t0 = time.monotonic()
        response = await anthropic_client.messages.create(
            model=model, max_tokens=1024, messages=messages
        )
        elapsed_ms = (time.monotonic() - t0) * 1000
        usage = response.usage
        cost = (
            usage.input_tokens  / 1_000_000 * INPUT_COST[model]
            + usage.output_tokens / 1_000_000 * OUTPUT_COST[model]
        )

        # Enrich span
        span.set_attribute("llm.input_tokens",  usage.input_tokens)
        span.set_attribute("llm.output_tokens", usage.output_tokens)
        span.set_attribute("llm.stop_reason",   response.stop_reason)
        span.set_attribute("llm.latency_ms",    int(elapsed_ms))
        span.set_attribute("llm.cost_usd",      round(cost, 6))

        # Emit metrics
        latency_hist.record(elapsed_ms, attributes={"llm.model": model})
        input_tok_ctr.add(usage.input_tokens,   attributes={"llm.model": model})
        output_tok_ctr.add(usage.output_tokens, attributes={"llm.model": model})
        cost_ctr.add(cost,                      attributes={"llm.model": model, "user.id": user_id})

        # Persist event row
        await record_telemetry_event(pool, {
            "conversation_id": conversation_id,
            "model": model,
            "input_tokens": usage.input_tokens,
            "output_tokens": usage.output_tokens,
            "latency_ms": int(elapsed_ms),
            "cost_usd": round(cost, 6),
            "stop_reason": response.stop_reason,
        })
        return response

CREATE TABLE llm_events (
    id               UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    conversation_id  UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES conversations(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    model            TEXT NOT NULL,
    input_tokens     INT  NOT NULL,
    output_tokens    INT  NOT NULL,
    latency_ms       INT  NOT NULL,
    cost_usd         NUMERIC(10, 6) NOT NULL,
    stop_reason      TEXT,
    created_at       TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Index for cost/token dashboards by model
CREATE INDEX llm_events_model_created ON llm_events (model, created_at DESC);
-- Index for per-conversation cost rollup
CREATE INDEX llm_events_conv ON llm_events (conversation_id);

async def record_telemetry_event(pool, event: dict) -> None:
    async with pool.acquire() as conn:
        await conn.execute(
            """INSERT INTO llm_events
                   (conversation_id, model, input_tokens, output_tokens,
                    latency_ms, cost_usd, stop_reason)
               VALUES ($1,$2,$3,$4,$5,$6,$7)""",
            event["conversation_id"], event["model"],
            event["input_tokens"],   event["output_tokens"],
            event["latency_ms"],     event["cost_usd"],
            event.get("stop_reason"),
        )

SELECT
    model,
    COUNT(*)                                         AS requests,
    ROUND(AVG(latency_ms))                           AS avg_ms,
    ROUND(PERCENTILE_CONT(0.95) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY latency_ms)) AS p95_ms,
    ROUND(PERCENTILE_CONT(0.99) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY latency_ms)) AS p99_ms
FROM llm_events
WHERE created_at > NOW() - INTERVAL '24 hours'
GROUP BY model
ORDER BY p95_ms DESC;

SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('day', created_at)  AS day,
    model,
    SUM(input_tokens)              AS total_input,
    SUM(output_tokens)             AS total_output,
    ROUND(SUM(cost_usd)::numeric, 4) AS total_cost_usd
FROM llm_events
WHERE created_at > NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days'
GROUP BY 1, 2
ORDER BY 1 DESC, 5 DESC;

SELECT
    e.conversation_id,
    c.user_id,
    COUNT(*)                         AS turns,
    SUM(e.input_tokens + e.output_tokens) AS total_tokens,
    ROUND(SUM(e.cost_usd)::numeric, 4)   AS total_cost_usd
FROM llm_events e
JOIN conversations c ON c.id = e.conversation_id
WHERE e.created_at > NOW() - INTERVAL '7 days'
GROUP BY e.conversation_id, c.user_id
ORDER BY total_cost_usd DESC
LIMIT 10;

For high-throughput deployments (thousands of requests per minute), TimescaleDB is more efficient than plain PostgreSQL for metric aggregations. It partitions data into time-ordered chunks and provides continuous aggregate views:

-- Convert llm_events to a hypertable (run once after table creation)
SELECT create_hypertable('llm_events', 'created_at');

-- Continuous aggregate: hourly token summary
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW llm_hourly_tokens
WITH (timescaledb.continuous) AS
SELECT
    TIME_BUCKET('1 hour', created_at) AS bucket,
    model,
    SUM(input_tokens)  AS input_tokens,
    SUM(output_tokens) AS output_tokens,
    SUM(cost_usd)      AS cost_usd,
    COUNT(*)           AS requests,
    AVG(latency_ms)    AS avg_latency_ms
FROM llm_events
GROUP BY bucket, model;

-- Refresh policy: recompute last 2 hours every hour
SELECT add_continuous_aggregate_policy('llm_hourly_tokens',
    start_offset => INTERVAL '2 hours',
    end_offset   => INTERVAL '1 hour',
    schedule_interval => INTERVAL '1 hour');
return in milliseconds even over months of data because TimescaleDB materialises them incrementally.
Structured logs attach context to every log line so they can be filtered and correlated in Loki or Elasticsearch:

import structlog
import logging

structlog.configure(
    processors=[
        structlog.contextvars.merge_contextvars,
        structlog.processors.add_log_level,
        structlog.processors.TimeStamper(fmt="iso"),
        structlog.processors.JSONRenderer(),
    ],
    logger_factory=structlog.PrintLoggerFactory(),
)

log = structlog.get_logger()

async def call_llm(pool, model, messages, user_id, conversation_id):
    log.info("llm.request.start", model=model, user_id=user_id, conversation_id=conversation_id)
    try:
        # ... call, record span, metrics ...
        log.info("llm.request.done",
                 model=model,
                 latency_ms=int(elapsed_ms),
                 input_tokens=usage.input_tokens,
                 output_tokens=usage.output_tokens,
                 cost_usd=round(cost, 6),
                 stop_reason=response.stop_reason)
        return response
    except Exception as exc:
        log.error("llm.request.error", model=model, error=str(exc), exc_info=True)
        raise
never log message content. Log only identifiers, token counts, latency, and error codes. The conversation text stays in the database with its own access controls.
Define alert conditions as SQL assertions against
Page on-call; check Anthropic status page
Slack warning; throttle or upgrade tier

# tests/test_telemetry.py
import pytest
from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, patch

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_call_llm_records_event(pg_pool, fake_anthropic_response):
    """llm_events row is written after a successful completion."""
    with patch("chat.anthropic_client.messages.create", return_value=fake_anthropic_response):
        await call_llm(
            pg_pool, "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
            [{"role": "user", "content": "hi"}],
            user_id="u1", conversation_id="c1",
        )

    row = await pg_pool.fetchrow(
        "SELECT * FROM llm_events WHERE conversation_id = 'c1'"
    )
    assert row["model"] == "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022"
    assert row["input_tokens"] == fake_anthropic_response.usage.input_tokens
    assert row["cost_usd"] > 0

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_call_llm_emits_metric(pg_pool, fake_anthropic_response, otel_reader):
    """Latency histogram receives one data point per call."""
    with patch("chat.anthropic_client.messages.create", return_value=fake_anthropic_response):
        await call_llm(pg_pool, "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022", [...], "u1", "c1")

    metrics = otel_reader.get_metrics_data()
    hist = next(m for m in metrics.resource_metrics[0].scope_metrics[0].metrics
                if m.name == "llm.request.duration_ms")
    assert hist.data.data_points[0].count == 1
Instrument with OpenTelemetry once at startup; FastAPIInstrumentor handles all routes automatically.
Emit LLM-specific span attributes (model, token counts, cost) and counter/histogram metrics on every completion.
Persist a raw event row in PostgreSQL for flexible ad-hoc analysis and billing export.
Upgrade to TimescaleDB and continuous aggregates for high-throughput deployments needing fast time-series queries.
Use structlog for structured JSON logs; never include message content.
Define alert thresholds against materialized metric tables so on-call engineers are paged on latency spikes, cost anomalies, and high error rates.
